title: The First Advent
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The First Advent - Pastor Tito Lyro - Luke 2_1-14.txt
outline: Caesar Augustus’ census and God’s sovereign providence (vv.1-5)
The birth of Jesus in Bethlehem (vv.6-7)
Angels appearing to shepherds with good news of great joy (vv.8-12)
The sign: a babe wrapped in swaddling cloths in a manger (v.12)
The meaning of the first advent for humanity
summary: An Advent/Christmas sermon on Luke 2:1-14, the first coming of Christ. (A separate sermon from ‘The Angels’ Song’ which covers the same passage.) Traces God’s sovereign hand working through Caesar’s census to bring Mary and Joseph to Bethlehem for the fulfillment of Messianic prophecy, and the announcement of the Savior’s birth to humble shepherds by the angelic host.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading — Luke 2:1–14

> *"And it came to pass in those days that a decree went out from Caesar Augustus that all the world should be registered. This census first took place while Quirinius was governing Syria. So all went to be registered, everyone to his own city. Joseph also went up from Galilee, out of the city of Nazareth, into Judea, to the city of David, which is called Bethlehem, because he was of the house and lineage of David, to be registered with Mary, his betrothed wife, who was with child. So it was, that while they were there, the days were completed for her to be delivered. And she brought forth her firstborn Son, and wrapped him in swaddling cloths, and laid him in a manger, because there was no room for them in the inn. Now there were in the same country shepherds living out in the fields, keeping watch over their flock by night. And behold, an angel of the Lord stood before them, and the glory of the Lord shone around them, and they were greatly afraid. Then the angel said to them, 'Do not be afraid, for behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy which will be to all people. For there is born to you this day in the city of David a Savior, who is Christ the Lord. And this will be the sign to you: You will find a Babe wrapped in swaddling cloths, lying in a manger.' And suddenly there was with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host praising God and saying: 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men!'"*

## The Song of the Angels

The story of Christ's birth in Luke is told through songs. Two weeks ago we saw Mary's Magnificat. Last week we heard Zechariah's Benedictus. Today we consider the song of the angels in response to the birth of Jesus — verse 14: "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, goodwill toward men."

As we look at this passage, don't let your familiarity rob you of its beauty. When you first hear "And it came to pass in those days that a decree went out from Caesar Augustus," your mind may immediately go to Linus reciting it in *A Charlie Brown Christmas*. That is how familiar we are with this passage. But let the familiarity go to the side. The same thing happens with sunsets. A resident who lives facing the Mediterranean eventually stops noticing the sunset. We must resist that with the Word of God.

**Christmas is essentially about the glory of God.** This is true because it is true of all of life — all of life is about the glory of God. We have been made to glorify him and enjoy him. So Christmas also must be about the glory of God.

## The Hand of God in History

The chapter opens with a wicked Roman emperor making a census decree for the purpose of taxation. Mary and Joseph have no earthly reason to leave Nazareth. And yet the prophet Micah had said:

> *"But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah, though you are little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of you shall come forth to me the one to be ruler in Israel."* (Micah 5:2)

God used Caesar Augustus to fulfill a prophecy. The hearts of emperors, kings, governors, and presidents are in the hand of the Lord, and he turns them whichever way he will.

## The Shepherds

When the greatest event in history occurred — the birth of the Savior of the world — to whom did the angel first appear? Not to Caesar. Not to the priests. To shepherds — the lowest possible group in society, considered liars and thieves, people no one wanted to be associated with. Right from birth, our Savior identified himself with the lowly. He was gentle and lowly, and it was to the lowly that the announcement of his birth came.

## What Is Glory?

The content of the angels' song is: "Glory to God in the highest." So what is glory?

In Hebrew, the word for glory (*kavod*) carries the idea of weight or heaviness. A person's importance was thought of in terms of weightiness. In Greek, *doxa* (from which we get "doxology") refers to the opinion or reputation one holds — in the highest sense, one's true worth.

Put together: **glory refers to a person's true importance.** The glory of God is the inescapable weight of his sheer goodness and holiness. God himself equates his glory with his goodness:

> *"[Moses said,] 'Please, show me your glory.' Then he said, 'I will make all my goodness pass before you.'"* (Exodus 33:18–19)

And in Isaiah 6, the seraphim cry:

> *"Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory!"* (Isaiah 6:3)

The glory of God is his weighty, inescapable goodness and holiness.

God gave his people concrete tokens of his glory throughout the ages: the cloud and pillar of fire in the wilderness, the Shekinah that filled the temple at its dedication. But the greatest demonstration of God's glory in these last days — and for all eternity — is **Jesus Christ himself**.

> *"And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth."* (John 1:14)

The word "dwelt" in John 1:14 is literally *pitched his tent* — tabernacled. What was the tabernacle in the Old Testament? The place where the people went to meet God. Jesus is that place where we meet God. He is the glory of the Father dwelling among us.

> *"[Christ] who being the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person."* (Hebrews 1:3)

You cannot know God as God unless you know him through faith in Jesus Christ. The Muslims do not worship the same God as we do. The Jehovah's Witnesses do not know the God of the Bible. The Jews do not worship the same God as we do. There is no other way to the Father except through the Son.

## How Do We Glorify God?

We cannot add to God's glory — he cannot become any more God than he already is. When we glorify him, we are not adding to his glory but **declaring** it. When we trust Jesus Christ for our salvation, we declare the glory of God. When we obey his word in faith, we declare that God is God and we are not.

We can only glorify God through Jesus Christ. Paul says in Ephesians 1 that God chose us, adopted us, and gave us every spiritual blessing — all "to the praise of his glorious grace." The glory of God is displayed when we worship together according to his word.

We glorify God when we obey Jesus because of our faith in him — not to earn his favor, but because we are "God's workmanship, created in Christ Jesus for good works" (Ephesians 2:10). Being a faithful missionary brings great glory to God — but so does being a godly spouse. Being willing to die for Christ brings great glory to God — but so does being a child who obeys his parents. A faithful pastor brings honor to God, but so does a faithful carpenter, school teacher, nurse, soldier, or accountant — when it is done through faith in Jesus Christ.

Do you know where God is most glorified in your life? Not in the big things. It is easy to try to do big things for the glory of God. It is Tuesday at 3 p.m. — the mundane moments of breakfast, figuring out the schedule, navigating ordinary relationships — that is where the glory of God is found. Thousands of little everyday moments that drive us to depend on the grace of God through Jesus Christ.

## The Shepherds' Response

Notice what the shepherds did once the angels declared "Glory to God in the highest." They said, "Let us now go to Bethlehem and see this thing that has come to pass" (v. 15). The glory of God drove them to Jesus.

The glory of God must drive us to Jesus. Jesus is the God-man who brings us together with God. He is the cleft in the rock that allows us to be in the presence of the glory of God and yet survive and benefit from it.

Hold on to the Jesus of the Bible — the Jesus of Christianity, the Jesus of God. Do not let anyone tell you there is another Christmas essential that is not anchored in him. And if you are not a believer, come into the presence of God's magnificent glory right now through faith in Jesus Christ. Today is the day of salvation. Embrace Christ and join the angels in singing: "Glory to God in the highest."
